intro:
Contentment (Greek autarkeia, self-sufficiency; arkeomai, to be satisfied) is the state of inward peace and sufficiency that rests on God rather than on circumstances or possessions. Paul's declaration in Philippians 4:11 — I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content — presents contentment as an acquired discipline rather than a natural disposition, learned through both abundance and want. The godly contentment described in 1 Timothy 6:6 is called great gain when combined with godliness, in contrast to the pursuit of material wealth as the path to security.

The writer of Hebrews links contentment directly to the character of God: be content with what you have, for he has said, I will never leave you nor forsake you (Hebrews 13:5), grounding the virtue in divine promise rather than Stoic self-sufficiency. The Old Testament background includes the provision of manna — given daily in sufficient measure with no allowance for hoarding — as a training in dependence on God's daily provision (Exodus 16:18). Proverbs 13:25 notes that the righteous has enough to satisfy his appetite. The NT consistently warns against the discontentment produced by covetousness and the love of money (James 3:16).
